FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone, no separate SM measure. B3 applies: 'Cyberbullying alone → NOT_MET'

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with prevalence ratios (PR) and 95% CI reported for cyberbullying → eating disorder symptoms

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis: cross-sectional secondary data analysis from 2-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in International Journal of Eating Disorders 2023; no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because cyberbullying is the INDEPENDENT variable (predictor), not the dependent variable. Eating disorder symptoms are the dependent variables. The paper tests cyberbullying → eating disorder outcome, not demographics → cyberbullying. Condition (a) fails.
notes: Paper excluded because it measures cyberbullying without a separately measured SM variable. Cyberbullying questions mention 'social media (like Instagram or Snapchat)' but cyberbullying is treated as a single construct; no separate SM use measurement.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study prospective follow-up from baseline 2016-2018

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (self-reported and parent-reported) as qualifying smartphone exposure. B1 applies: 'time on phone (self-report, parent-report, EARS)'

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects models to analyze associations between baseline screen time and mental health outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract only mentions 'mixed-effects models' but no numeric values (beta, p, CI) extractable from abstract

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis: prospective follow-up with mixed-effects models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published prospective study; no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because screen time is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting mental health (dependent variable). The paper tests screen time → mental health outcome, not demographics → screen time. Condition (a) fails.
notes: Cannot fully assess without PDF. Based on abstract, meets IC1-3, IC5-6 but IC4 UNKNOWN due to lack of numeric values in abstract alone. Likely MET after full-text review if beta/coefficient values present in main text or tables.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract mentions 'ABCD study' and 'ABCD-youth' but does not explicitly confirm US ABCD Study or clarify if this is using ABCD data vs. citation only. Requires full text confirmation.

FT_IC2.evidence: Problematic social media use and problematic gaming measured. Qualifying SM variable present. B6 likely applies: 'Mobile/app gaming or gaming + SM with separate coefficients → MET'

FT_IC3.evidence: Bidirectional associations tested; likely mixed-effects or structural equation modeling implied by title reference to 'associations'

FT_IC4.evidence: PDF available but abstract only provided in batch. Full text required to extract numeric values (beta, OR, correlation coefficients)

FT_IC5.evidence: Abstract suggests empirical analysis but unclear if original ABCD analysis or secondary analysis of existing data without new coefficients

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate markers evident but insufficient information in abstract

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Title indicates bidirectional associations (B20): SM ↔ mental health. Bidirectional/cross-lagged models disqualify FT-EC7 per binding rule B20: 'If bidirectional / cross-lagged (B20): FT-EC7 does NOT apply.'
notes: Full-text review required to confirm: (1) uses original ABCD data (not just citation); (2) extraction of numeric coefficients for IC4; (3) confirmation of bidirectional design. PDF present but only abstract reviewed in batch data.
